subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 6-month-old infant is brought with failure to thrive. On examination, there is a harsh pansystolic murmur at the lower left sternal border with a palpable systolic thrill. Echocardiography confirms a perimembranous ventricular septal defect. Which of the following statements is CORRECT regarding this condition?
options: VSD is the most common cyanotic congenital heart defect
VSD is the most common congenital heart defect overall, accounting for 30-35% of all CHDs
Systolic thrill is more commonly associated with small VSDs
Perimembranous VSDs have a higher rate of spontaneous closure than muscular VSDs
explanation: VSD (Ventricular Septal Defect) is the MOST COMMON congenital heart defect overall, accounting for approximately 30-35% of all CHDs. It is an acyanotic left-to-right shunt. The harsh pansystolic murmur at the lower left sternal border (LLSB) with a systolic thrill is characteristic. Muscular VSDs have a higher rate of spontaneous closure compared to perimembranous VSDs. Small VSDs produce loud murmurs (Maladie de Roger) due to high turbulence through a small orifice, but the thrill is associated with significant turbulence which is seen in moderate-sized VSDs. VSD is NOT a cyanotic CHD unless Eisenmenger syndrome develops.
highYield: VSD = most common CHD overall (30-35%); harsh pansystolic murmur at LLSB with systolic thrill; muscular VSDs close spontaneously more often than perimembranous
pyqNote: Frequently asked in NEET PG: VSD being the most common CHD and its murmur characteristics

subject: Pediatrics
topic: Congenital Heart Disease - VSD (Maladie de Roger)
stem: A 2-year-old child is incidentally found to have a very loud, harsh pansystolic murmur at the lower left sternal border during a routine checkup. The child is thriving well with no symptoms of heart failure. Echocardiography shows a tiny muscular VSD with a high-velocity jet. Which eponym correctly describes this presentation?
explanation: Maladie de Roger (Roger's disease) describes a small, restrictive VSD that produces a loud harsh pansystolic murmur despite being clinically insignificant hemodynamically. This paradox occurs because the high pressure difference between the left and right ventricles through a tiny orifice creates maximum turbulence. The child has no symptoms because the shunt volume is small, but the murmur is disproportionately loud. Lutembacher syndrome is ASD + mitral stenosis. Eisenmenger syndrome is reversal of shunt due to pulmonary hypertension. These children usually thrive well and many tiny muscular VSDs close spontaneously.
highYield: Maladie de Roger = small VSD producing disproportionately LOUD murmur; child is asymptomatic; high chance of spontaneous closure
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG eponym question — small VSD with loud murmur = Maladie de Roger

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 22-year-old woman with a known large unrepaired VSD since childhood presents with progressive dyspnea and cyanosis. On examination, she has clubbing, central cyanosis, and a soft systolic murmur (previously harsh). Echocardiography shows right-to-left shunting across the VSD with severe pulmonary arterial hypertension. Which of the following is the MOST appropriate intervention?
explanation: This patient has developed Eisenmenger syndrome - reversal of the original left-to-right shunt to right-to-left when pulmonary resistance (Rp) equals or exceeds systemic resistance (Lp). Key features: cyanosis, clubbing, polycythemia, and softening of the previously loud murmur (reduced shunt volume). The original harsh murmur becomes soft because there is less turbulent flow across the VSD. Once Eisenmenger syndrome develops, surgical closure of the VSD is CONTRAINDICATED as the VSD is now acting as a 'pop-off valve' for the right ventricle. The only definitive treatment is heart-lung transplantation. Pregnancy is also contraindicated in Eisenmenger syndrome (mortality ~30-50%).
highYield: Eisenmenger = L→R reverses to R→L when Rp=Lp; soft murmur replaces harsh murmur; VSD closure CONTRAINDICATED; heart-lung transplant only option; pregnancy contraindicated
pyqNote: High-yield NEET PG: Eisenmenger management and why surgical closure is contraindicated

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 10-year-old girl presents for evaluation of a heart murmur. On auscultation, there is fixed wide splitting of the second heart sound (S2) with an ejection systolic murmur in the pulmonary area. ECG shows right axis deviation with incomplete RBBB. Which is the MOST COMMON type of ASD?
explanation: Ostium secundum ASD is the MOST COMMON type of ASD (70-75% of all ASDs), located in the region of the fossa ovalis. The hallmark auscultatory finding of ASD is FIXED WIDE SPLITTING of S2 - this is because the right ventricle has increased volume load causing delayed pulmonary valve closure, and this delay does not vary with respiration (fixed). The ejection systolic murmur in the pulmonary area is due to increased flow across the pulmonary valve (not the ASD itself). ECG shows right axis deviation with incomplete RBBB (rSR' pattern in V1). Ostium primum ASD (endocardial cushion defect) is associated with Down syndrome and left axis deviation.
highYield: Secundum ASD = most common (70-75%); FIXED WIDE SPLITTING of S2 = hallmark; ESM at pulmonary area from increased flow; ECG = right axis deviation + incomplete RBBB
pyqNote: Most frequently asked ASD fact in NEET PG: fixed wide splitting of S2 and most common type being secundum

subject: Pediatrics
topic: Congenital Heart Disease - Ostium Primum ASD
stem: A child with Down syndrome undergoes echocardiography which reveals an ASD in the lower portion of the atrial septum with a cleft mitral valve. Which of the following ECG findings would most likely be present?
options: Right axis deviation with incomplete RBBB
Left axis deviation with incomplete RBBB
Right axis deviation with complete RBBB
Normal axis with tall P waves
explanation: Ostium primum ASD (also called partial AV canal or endocardial cushion defect) is located in the lower portion of the atrial septum, near the AV valves. It is the type of ASD associated with Down syndrome and almost always has a cleft anterior mitral leaflet. The characteristic ECG finding is LEFT AXIS DEVIATION (due to the position of the defect affecting left bundle branch conduction) combined with incomplete RBBB pattern. This combination of left axis deviation with RBBB (superior QRS axis) distinguishes ostium primum from ostium secundum (which shows right axis deviation + incomplete RBBB). Complete AV canal (AVSD) is the most common CHD in Down syndrome overall.
highYield: Ostium primum ASD = endocardial cushion defect; Down syndrome association; cleft mitral valve; ECG = LEFT axis deviation + incomplete RBBB (distinguishes from secundum ASD)
pyqNote: Key differentiator: primum ASD → left axis deviation (vs. secundum → right axis deviation); Down syndrome link

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A premature neonate born at 28 weeks gestation develops respiratory distress at 48 hours of life. On examination, there are bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure, and a continuous machinery murmur heard best at the left infraclavicular area. Which is the FIRST-LINE pharmacological treatment?
explanation: Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA) in premature infants is described by the continuous 'machinery murmur' heard at the left infraclavicular area (Gibson murmur), bounding pulses, and wide pulse pressure due to the aorta-to-pulmonary artery shunt during both systole and diastole. INDOMETHACIN (a prostaglandin synthetase inhibitor/NSAID) is the first-line pharmacological treatment for PDA closure in premature infants. It inhibits prostaglandin synthesis, allowing the ductus to close. If indomethacin fails or is contraindicated, surgical ligation is performed. Ibuprofen is an alternative. PGE1 (prostaglandin) is used to KEEP the ductus OPEN in duct-dependent CHDs (e.g., TGA, critical pulmonary stenosis, HLHS). Propranolol is used for tet spells in TOF.
highYield: PDA = premature infant; continuous machinery murmur (Gibson) at left infraclavicular; bounding pulses + wide pulse pressure; Indomethacin = pharmacological closure; surgery if failed
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG: PDA treatment — indomethacin first, then surgery; PGE1 is to OPEN ductus (opposite purpose)

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 3-year-old child is found to have a PDA. The parents ask about the murmur characteristics. Which of the following CORRECTLY describes the timing and location of the PDA murmur?
options: Pansystolic murmur at the lower left sternal border
Continuous (systolic + diastolic) machinery murmur at left infraclavicular area, peaking at S2
Ejection systolic murmur at the aortic area with radiation to carotids
explanation: The PDA murmur is a continuous 'machinery' murmur that spans both systole and diastole, peaking at the second heart sound (S2). It is heard best at the left infraclavicular area (below the left clavicle). The continuity through S2 is the key feature distinguishing it from other murmurs. The machinery quality is due to turbulent flow from the high-pressure aorta to the lower-pressure pulmonary artery throughout the cardiac cycle. A pansystolic murmur at LLSB is VSD. Mid-diastolic at apex is mitral stenosis or Austin-Flint murmur. Ejection systolic at aortic area radiating to carotids is aortic stenosis.
highYield: PDA murmur = continuous machinery murmur; peaks at S2 (unlike venous hum which obliterates with neck pressure); best heard at left infraclavicular area
pyqNote: Differentiation of continuous murmurs is high-yield: PDA (left infraclavicular, peaks at S2) vs venous hum (obliterates with pressure)

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 5-year-old boy is evaluated for a heart murmur. On auscultation, an ejection click is heard at the pulmonary area followed by an ejection systolic murmur. ECG shows right ventricular hypertrophy. Echocardiography reveals a peak gradient of 55 mmHg across the pulmonary valve. Which is the MOST APPROPRIATE treatment?
explanation: Pulmonary stenosis (PS) presents with an ejection click (high-frequency click due to sudden opening of the domed pulmonary valve) followed by an ejection systolic murmur (ESM) at the pulmonary area. The ejection click is characteristic of valvular PS. ECG shows RVH. The treatment decision is based on the gradient: if the peak gradient is >40 mmHg (or mean gradient >30 mmHg), intervention is indicated. BALLOON PULMONARY VALVULOPLASTY (percutaneous balloon dilatation) is the treatment of choice for valvular PS. It has excellent results with low complication rates. Surgical valvotomy is reserved for dysplastic valves (as in Noonan syndrome) where balloon valvuloplasty is less effective. A gradient of 55 mmHg clearly exceeds the 40 mmHg threshold for intervention.
highYield: PS = ejection click + ESM at pulmonary area; RVH on ECG; gradient >40 mmHg → balloon pulmonary valvuloplasty (treatment of choice); surgery for dysplastic valves (Noonan syndrome)
pyqNote: NEET PG frequently tests threshold for PS intervention: >40 mmHg gradient → balloon valvuloplasty

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 2-year-old child with known Tetralogy of Fallot suddenly develops severe cyanosis, hyperpnea, and loss of consciousness during crying. The murmur has disappeared. Which of the following is the IMMEDIATE management?
explanation: This is a hypercyanotic spell (Tet spell) in TOF. During a tet spell, the RVOT spasm increases, shunting more deoxygenated blood right-to-left through the VSD, causing severe cyanosis. The murmur disappears because there is no flow across the RVOT. Management: (1) Knee-chest position (increases SVR by compressing femoral arteries, reducing R→L shunt), (2) Morphine (calms infant, reduces hyperpnea and tachycardia), (3) IV propranolol (relaxes RVOT spasm), (4) Oxygen, (5) IV fluids, (6) Sodium bicarbonate for acidosis. Squatting in older children has the same mechanism as knee-chest position - increasing SVR. Emergency surgery is not the immediate treatment. PGE1 is for duct-dependent lesions. Diuretics are not indicated (may worsen hypovolemia).
highYield: Tet spell = hypercyanotic episode; disappearing murmur; knee-chest position → increases SVR; morphine + propranolol; squatting = older child equivalent
pyqNote: Extremely high-yield NEET PG: management of tet spell — knee-chest position is the key immediate measure

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 1-year-old cyanotic child with TOF is awaiting complete repair. The chest X-ray shows a boot-shaped heart (coeur en sabot). Which of the following is the correct set of four components of Tetralogy of Fallot?
options: ASD, RVH, overriding pulmonary artery, right aortic arch
VSD, pulmonary stenosis, overriding aorta, right ventricular hypertrophy
VSD, ASD, overriding aorta, left ventricular hypertrophy
explanation: Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) consists of four components: (1) Ventricular Septal Defect (VSD - large, subarterial), (2) Pulmonary Stenosis (RVOT obstruction, the 'driving force' of the disease), (3) Overriding Aorta (aorta straddles the VSD), (4) Right Ventricular Hypertrophy (secondary to PS). TOF is the MOST COMMON CYANOTIC congenital heart disease. The boot-shaped heart on CXR (coeur en sabot) is due to upturned cardiac apex from RVH + absent/small pulmonary artery shadow. Pulmonary oligemia is seen on CXR. Right aortic arch is present in 25% of TOF cases. The fundamental defect is maldevelopment of the infundibular septum.
highYield: TOF = most common CYANOTIC CHD; 4 components = VSD + PS + overriding aorta + RVH; boot-shaped heart (coeur en sabot) on CXR; right aortic arch in 25%
pyqNote: Most commonly tested: 4 components of TOF and 'most common cyanotic CHD' — must know both

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A child with cyanotic congenital heart disease undergoes chest X-ray. The X-ray shows a boot-shaped cardiac silhouette with decreased pulmonary vascular markings. Additionally, the aortic knob appears on the right side. What is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis and what percentage of cases of this condition show a right aortic arch?
options: Truncus arteriosus - right aortic arch in 50%
Ebstein anomaly - right aortic arch in 10%
explanation: The boot-shaped heart (coeur en sabot) with decreased pulmonary vascular markings (pulmonary oligemia) is pathognomonic for Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF). The right aortic arch is present in approximately 25% of TOF cases (clinically important as it affects surgical approach). The boot shape results from: upturned cardiac apex due to RVH + small/absent main pulmonary artery segment + concavity in the upper left cardiac border. Pulmonary oligemia differentiates TOF from TGA (which shows increased pulmonary vascularity with 'egg on side' appearance). Truncus arteriosus shows increased pulmonary flow. Ebstein anomaly shows a huge 'sail-shaped' heart.
highYield: TOF CXR: boot-shaped heart + pulmonary oligemia + right aortic arch (25%); right aortic arch affects surgical planning
pyqNote: NEET PG commonly asks about CXR findings in cyanotic CHDs — memorize the characteristic CXR for each

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 4-month-old infant with TOF has severe hypoxemia and is too small for complete intracardiac repair. The surgeon decides to perform a palliative shunt procedure to increase pulmonary blood flow. Which procedure involves anastomosis of the subclavian artery to the ipsilateral pulmonary artery?
explanation: The Blalock-Taussig (BT) shunt is the most commonly used palliative procedure for TOF. In the CLASSIC BT shunt, the subclavian artery is anastomosed end-to-side to the ipsilateral pulmonary artery to increase pulmonary blood flow. The MODIFIED BT shunt uses a Goretex tube interposed between the subclavian artery and pulmonary artery (more commonly used today). The Waterston shunt connects the ascending aorta to the right pulmonary artery. The Potts shunt connects the descending aorta to the left pulmonary artery. The Glenn shunt connects the superior vena cava to the pulmonary artery (used in univentricular heart palliation, step 2 of Fontan). Complete repair involves VSD closure + RVOT patch.
highYield: BT shunt = subclavian artery to pulmonary artery; palliation for TOF when too small for complete repair; complete repair = VSD closure + RVOT patch
pyqNote: Surgical procedures in TOF: BT shunt (palliation) vs complete repair; also know Waterston, Potts, Glenn for distinction

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A cyanotic neonate presents at 2 days of life with severe central cyanosis. CXR shows an 'egg on side' or 'egg on string' cardiac silhouette with increased pulmonary vascular markings. Echocardiography confirms parallel circulation. Which of the following is the MOST APPROPRIATE EMERGENCY measure?
options: Surgical Jatene arterial switch operation immediately
PGE1 infusion to maintain ductal patency + Rashkind balloon atrial septostomy
explanation: D-Transposition of Great Arteries (D-TGA) creates PARALLEL circulations: the aorta arises from the right ventricle (carrying deoxygenated blood to the body) and the pulmonary artery from the left ventricle (carrying oxygenated blood back to the lungs). This is INCOMPATIBLE WITH LIFE unless there is mixing. Emergency management: (1) PGE1 (Prostaglandin E1) infusion to keep the ductus arteriosus open for mixing, (2) Rashkind balloon atrial septostomy (emergency procedure to create/enlarge an ASD for better mixing). Definitive surgery is the Jatene ARTERIAL SWITCH operation (ASO) - reconnects aorta to LV and PA to RV, with reimplantation of coronary arteries. This should be done within the first 2-4 weeks of life. The 'egg on side/string' CXR is due to narrow superior mediastinum (parallel great vessels).
highYield: TGA = parallel circulations; 'egg on side' CXR; PGE1 to keep ductus open + Rashkind balloon septostomy (emergency); Jatene arterial switch = definitive surgery within 2-4 weeks
pyqNote: High-yield NEET PG: TGA management sequence — PGE1 → Rashkind → Jatene; 'egg on side' CXR is classic

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A neonate with D-TGA is stabilized with PGE1. Emergency Rashkind balloon atrial septostomy is planned. What is the physiological basis for using balloon atrial septostomy in TGA?
options: It creates communication between parallel circulations allowing mixing of oxygenated and deoxygenated blood
It converts TGA to a left-to-right shunt
It increases pulmonary blood flow by reducing pulmonary vascular resistance
explanation: In D-TGA, there are two separate (parallel) circuits: the systemic circuit carries deoxygenated blood (aorta from RV → body → SVC/IVC → RA → RV) and the pulmonary circuit carries oxygenated blood (PA from LV → lungs → pulmonary veins → LA → LV). For survival, there MUST be mixing of these parallel circuits. Rashkind balloon atrial septostomy creates or enlarges an opening at the atrial septum (by tearing the foramen ovale with a balloon catheter). This allows bidirectional mixing of blood at the atrial level - some oxygenated blood from the LA can enter the RA and reach the aorta, improving systemic oxygen saturation. PGE1 maintains the ductus as another mixing site. Without any mixing, TGA is rapidly fatal. The septostomy buys time until the Jatene operation can be performed.
highYield: Rashkind BAS = creates ASD for mixing between parallel circuits in TGA; bidirectional atrial-level mixing improves systemic saturation; emergency bedside procedure
pyqNote: Understanding physiology behind Rashkind procedure is often tested in NEET PG clinical reasoning questions

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A neonate presents with cyanosis and a massively enlarged heart on CXR described as 'sail-shaped' or 'box-shaped'. ECG shows right bundle branch block and tall peaked P waves. Echocardiography reveals apical displacement of the tricuspid valve leaflets with 'atrialization' of the right ventricle. Which arrhythmia is CLASSICALLY associated with this condition?
explanation: Ebstein anomaly is characterized by downward (apical) displacement of the tricuspid valve leaflets into the right ventricle, 'atrialization' of the RV (the proximal RV becomes part of the RA), and a sail-shaped or box-shaped massive cardiomegaly on CXR. It is almost always associated with an ASD (allowing right-to-left shunting causing cyanosis). The CLASSIC ASSOCIATED ARRHYTHMIA is Wolff-Parkinson-White (WPW) syndrome due to accessory pathways (multiple bypass tracts are common). The ECG shows RBBB, tall peaked P waves (Himalayan P waves), prolonged PR interval. The CXR appearance of massive cardiomegaly is due to the enormous right atrium. Lithium exposure during pregnancy is a risk factor for Ebstein anomaly.
highYield: Ebstein anomaly = apical displacement of tricuspid leaflets; ASD usually present; WPW association; sail/box-shaped heart CXR; lithium exposure risk factor
pyqNote: NEET PG: Ebstein anomaly = WPW association + sail-shaped heart + lithium exposure; apical displacement of tricuspid is the pathological hallmark

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A neonate is born at term and develops rapid deterioration with shock and severe acidosis at 48-72 hours of life. Echocardiography reveals a severely hypoplastic left ventricle, mitral atresia, and aortic atresia with a hypoplastic ascending aorta. PGE1 is started. What is the staged surgical palliation planned for this child?
options: Jatene arterial switch → Rastelli procedure → Fontan
Norwood procedure → Glenn shunt → Fontan completion
BT shunt → Mustard procedure → heart transplant
explanation: Hypoplastic Left Heart Syndrome (HLHS) is a duct-dependent lesion where the systemic circulation depends on the patent ductus arteriosus (hence PGE1 is critical). HLHS includes hypoplastic LV, mitral atresia/stenosis, aortic atresia/stenosis, and hypoplastic ascending aorta. The staged surgical palliation (Norwood palliation) involves: Stage 1 (Norwood procedure, neonatal): reconstructs neo-aorta from pulmonary artery, creates a source of pulmonary blood flow (BT shunt or Sano shunt), atrial septectomy. Stage 2 (Glenn shunt, 4-6 months): SVC to pulmonary artery anastomosis, removes one source of pulmonary flow. Stage 3 (Fontan completion, 2-4 years): IVC connected to pulmonary artery, RV pumps systemic circulation. Heart transplantation is an alternative for Stage 1. This is a univentricular heart palliation pathway.
highYield: HLHS = duct-dependent; PGE1 critical; Norwood stages: 1=Norwood procedure (neonatal) → 2=Glenn shunt (4-6 months) → 3=Fontan completion (2-4 years)
pyqNote: NEET PG: HLHS management sequence — Norwood → Glenn → Fontan; PGE1 to keep ductus open is the immediate measure

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A neonate presents with central cyanosis and congestive heart failure. CXR shows cardiomegaly with increased pulmonary vascular markings. Echocardiography reveals a single great vessel arising from the heart that gives rise to the aorta, pulmonary arteries, and coronary arteries. A large VSD is also present. Which of the following is ALWAYS present in truncus arteriosus?
explanation: Persistent Truncus Arteriosus (PTA/Common Arterial Trunk) results from failure of the aortopulmonary septum to divide the primitive truncus into separate aorta and pulmonary artery. A single great vessel (truncus) arises from the heart and gives rise to the aorta, pulmonary arteries, and coronary arteries. A VSD (ventricular septal defect) is ALWAYS PRESENT in truncus arteriosus - it is the outlet VSD through which the single truncal vessel 'rides.' Without a VSD, blood from both ventricles cannot enter the single truncus. CXR shows increased pulmonary vascularity (increased flow). Right aortic arch is seen in ~30-35% (not always). DiGeorge syndrome (22q11 deletion) is strongly associated with truncus arteriosus.
highYield: Truncus arteriosus = single great vessel from heart; VSD ALWAYS present; failure of aortopulmonary separation; DiGeorge syndrome (22q11) association; increased pulmonary flow on CXR
pyqNote: Classic NEET PG fact: VSD is ALWAYS present in truncus arteriosus; also DiGeorge association

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A neonate presents with severe cyanosis, respiratory distress, and marked pulmonary venous congestion on CXR. Echocardiography reveals that ALL four pulmonary veins drain into the right atrium via a common vertical vein, with obstruction at the entry point. Which of the following is MOST CORRECT?
options: This is unobstructed TAPVC and can be managed electively
This is obstructed TAPVC and requires emergency surgical repair
PGE1 should be administered and surgery deferred for 3 months
Balloon septostomy alone will be curative
explanation: Total Anomalous Pulmonary Venous Connection (TAPVC) occurs when all pulmonary veins drain into the right-sided circulation instead of the left atrium. Types: supracardiac (most common, drains to SVC/innominate), cardiac (to coronary sinus/RA), infracardiac (to portal vein/IVC - MOST LIKELY TO BE OBSTRUCTED), mixed. OBSTRUCTED TAPVC (as in infracardiac type) is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY - the pulmonary venous obstruction causes severe pulmonary edema, refractory hypoxia, and pulmonary hypertension. CXR shows 'snowman/figure-of-8' in supracardiac type; obstructed TAPVC shows pulmonary edema/ground glass. ASD is almost always present for any systemic output. Emergency surgical repair (rerouting pulmonary veins to LA) is mandatory. PGE1 has limited/no role in obstructed TAPVC and may worsen pulmonary edema.
highYield: TAPVC: all pulmonary veins drain to right side; obstructed TAPVC = surgical EMERGENCY; infracardiac type most likely obstructed; snowman CXR in supracardiac type; ASD always present for survival
pyqNote: NEET PG: obstructed TAPVC is one of the few cardiac surgical emergencies in neonates; 'snowman' CXR pattern for supracardiac TAPVC

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 25-year-old man with a history of large unrepaired ASD since childhood develops Eisenmenger syndrome. Which of the following is NOT a feature of Eisenmenger syndrome?
explanation: Eisenmenger syndrome results from reversal of an originally left-to-right shunt (VSD, ASD, PDA) to right-to-left due to severe pulmonary arterial hypertension (when pulmonary resistance equals or exceeds systemic resistance). Features include: (1) Cyanosis - due to R→L shunting of deoxygenated blood, (2) Polycythemia - secondary erythrocytosis due to chronic hypoxia, (3) Digital clubbing - due to chronic hypoxia, (4) Loud P2, signs of pulmonary hypertension. PARADOXICAL SPLITTING of S2 is NOT a feature of Eisenmenger syndrome. Paradoxical splitting occurs in conditions with delayed LV emptying (LBBB, aortic stenosis). Fixed wide splitting of S2 was a feature of the original ASD before Eisenmenger developed. In Eisenmenger, the S2 may show loud P2 (due to pulmonary hypertension) with fixed/single splitting. Pregnancy is contraindicated (maternal mortality ~30-50%). Transplant is the only definitive option.
highYield: Eisenmenger = cyanosis + polycythemia + clubbing; reversal of L→R to R→L when Rp≥Lp; paradoxical splitting of S2 is NOT a feature; heart-lung transplant = only option; pregnancy contraindicated
pyqNote: Negative question format common in NEET PG — test knowledge of what is NOT present in Eisenmenger syndrome

subject: Pediatrics
topic: Congenital Heart Disease - Eisenmenger and Pregnancy
stem: A 28-year-old woman with Eisenmenger syndrome (from a large ASD) discovers she is 8 weeks pregnant. She has cyanosis, clubbing, and resting oxygen saturation of 82%. What is the MOST APPROPRIATE advice?
options: Pregnancy can continue with close monitoring and oxygen therapy
Termination of pregnancy is strongly advised due to very high maternal mortality
Emergency VSD/ASD repair can now be performed to allow safe pregnancy
Bosentan therapy alone will allow safe continuation of pregnancy
explanation: Eisenmenger syndrome is an ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION to pregnancy. Maternal mortality in Eisenmenger syndrome during pregnancy and the peripartum period is approximately 30-50%. Reasons: (1) The physiological fall in systemic vascular resistance (SVR) during pregnancy increases right-to-left shunting, worsening cyanosis, (2) The hypercoagulable state of pregnancy increases thrombosis risk (fatal pulmonary thrombosis), (3) Hemodynamic changes of labor are poorly tolerated. Termination of pregnancy is strongly advised early in gestation. Surgical repair of the defect at this stage is CONTRAINDICATED (the VSD/ASD is acting as a safety valve for the RV - closing it would cause acute RV failure and death). Bosentan (pulmonary vasodilator) is teratogenic and does not make pregnancy safe. Fetal mortality is also very high.
highYield: Eisenmenger syndrome: pregnancy CONTRAINDICATED; maternal mortality 30-50%; termination strongly advised; surgical closure CONTRAINDICATED (ASD/VSD = safety valve for RV)
pyqNote: NEET PG: Eisenmenger = absolute contraindication to pregnancy; maternal mortality ~30-50% is frequently tested

subject: Pediatrics
topic: Congenital Heart Disease - VSD Spontaneous Closure
stem: Parents of a 6-month-old infant with a muscular VSD ask about the chances of spontaneous closure. The pediatric cardiologist counsels them. Which of the following is the MOST ACCURATE statement regarding spontaneous closure of VSD?
options: Perimembranous VSDs close spontaneously more often than muscular VSDs
Muscular VSDs have a higher rate of spontaneous closure than perimembranous VSDs
Spontaneous closure never occurs after 2 years of age
Outlet (supracristal) VSDs have the highest rate of spontaneous closure
explanation: Muscular VSDs have a HIGHER rate of spontaneous closure compared to perimembranous VSDs. Muscular VSDs can close spontaneously as the ventricular muscle hypertrophies and the muscular rim grows around the defect. Spontaneous closure occurs in: muscular VSDs >80%, perimembranous VSDs ~40-50%. Spontaneous closure can occur up to age 5-6 years (not just before 2 years). Outlet/supracristal (subarterial) VSDs have the LOWEST rate of spontaneous closure and are also prone to aortic regurgitation (due to prolapse of right aortic cusp), requiring earlier surgical repair. The mechanism of spontaneous closure in perimembranous VSDs is often through aneurysm formation of the membranous septum.
highYield: Muscular VSD > perimembranous VSD for spontaneous closure rate; outlet/supracristal VSD = lowest closure rate + aortic regurgitation risk; closure can occur up to 5-6 years
pyqNote: NEET PG: spontaneous closure rates — muscular > perimembranous > outlet; outlet VSD has least chance of closing

subject: Pediatrics
topic: Congenital Heart Disease - ASD vs VSD Differentiation
stem: A 12-year-old girl is found to have a cardiac defect on routine examination. Auscultation reveals wide fixed splitting of S2 with a soft ejection systolic murmur at the upper left sternal border. There is no systolic thrill. Which is the MOST LIKELY diagnosis?
explanation: The key findings pointing to ASD: (1) FIXED WIDE SPLITTING of S2 - the hallmark of ASD (the right ventricle has increased volume load regardless of respiratory phase, so P2 closure is equally delayed in both inspiration and expiration - hence 'fixed'); (2) Soft ejection systolic murmur at upper left sternal border - this is due to increased flow across the PULMONARY VALVE (not across the ASD itself, which is silent); (3) No systolic thrill - ASD typically does not produce a thrill because the flow across the ASD is laminar and low velocity. In VSD: harsh pansystolic murmur at LLSB with systolic thrill is characteristic. PDA: continuous machinery murmur. PS: ejection click + ESM with possible thrill but splitting is not fixed (P2 is delayed). The ESM in ASD is a 'relative pulmonary stenosis' due to increased flow.
highYield: ASD hallmark = fixed wide splitting of S2; ESM from increased pulmonary valve flow (not across ASD itself); no thrill (unlike VSD); upper left sternal border
pyqNote: Classic differentiating question: fixed wide splitting = ASD; harsh pansystolic + thrill = VSD; machinery murmur = PDA

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 3-year-old boy with Tetralogy of Fallot is noted to squat repeatedly during play. His mother reports that whenever he becomes cyanotic during exertion, he squats down and the cyanosis improves. What is the physiological mechanism of squatting in TOF?
options: Squatting decreases venous return from lower limbs, reducing cardiac output
Squatting compresses femoral arteries, increases systemic vascular resistance, reducing right-to-left shunt
Squatting directly relieves RVOT obstruction by stretching the infundibulum
Squatting decreases pulmonary vascular resistance, improving oxygenation
explanation: Squatting is a compensatory mechanism in TOF patients. When squatting, the femoral arteries in the groin are mechanically compressed, increasing the SYSTEMIC VASCULAR RESISTANCE (SVR). When SVR increases, the pressure difference between the left ventricle and the right ventricle changes - it becomes harder for blood to shunt from RV to LV (through the VSD). This REDUCES the right-to-left shunt across the VSD, meaning more blood goes through the RVOT into the pulmonary circulation, improving oxygenation. Additionally, squatting traps deoxygenated blood in the lower limbs, sending it back to the heart more slowly. In acute tet spells: the knee-chest position in infants achieves the same effect (increases SVR by compressing abdominal aorta and femoral vessels). Propranolol works by relaxing the RVOT spasm.
highYield: Squatting in TOF = increases SVR (femoral artery compression) → reduces R→L shunt → more blood to lungs → improved oxygenation; same mechanism as knee-chest position in infants
pyqNote: One of the most frequently tested physiology questions in NEET PG pediatrics: mechanism of squatting in TOF

subject: Pediatrics
topic: Congenital Heart Disease - PDA in Preterm
stem: A preterm neonate at 28 weeks gestation is on mechanical ventilation for RDS. On day 3, the respiratory status suddenly worsens. Physical examination reveals active precordium, bounding pulses, and a to-and-fro murmur. Which of the following is the CORRECT statement about management?
options: Indomethacin is preferred over ibuprofen as it does not affect renal function
Ibuprofen and indomethacin are both COX inhibitors that can close the PDA; ibuprofen has less renal side effects
PGE1 should be given to promote ductal closure
explanation: In premature infants with hemodynamically significant PDA (worsening respiratory status, active precordium, bounding pulses, wide pulse pressure), pharmacological closure is attempted with COX inhibitors (prostaglandin synthesis inhibitors). Both INDOMETHACIN and IBUPROFEN are COX inhibitors that inhibit prostaglandin synthesis and close the PDA. The key difference: Indomethacin causes more renal vasoconstriction and has more side effects (oliguria, platelet dysfunction, NEC risk). IBUPROFEN has a comparable closure rate with FEWER RENAL SIDE EFFECTS (preferred in infants with borderline renal function). PGE1 is used to OPEN the ductus (opposite purpose - contraindicated here). If pharmacological closure fails, surgical ligation is performed. Oral paracetamol (acetaminophen) is an emerging alternative that inhibits the peroxidase component of COX.
highYield: PDA closure: indomethacin vs ibuprofen (both COX inhibitors); ibuprofen = fewer renal side effects; PGE1 keeps ductus OPEN (don't confuse); surgical ligation if pharmacotherapy fails
pyqNote: NEET PG: ibuprofen vs indomethacin for PDA — both effective but ibuprofen has fewer renal side effects; PGE1 is the OPPOSITE (opens ductus)

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A neonate with TAPVC is brought with severe respiratory distress. CXR shows a 'snowman' or 'figure-of-8' silhouette. Which TYPE of TAPVC corresponds to this CXR finding, and where do the pulmonary veins drain in this type?
options: Infracardiac TAPVC - pulmonary veins drain to portal vein/ductus venosus
Supracardiac TAPVC - pulmonary veins drain to left vertical vein → innominate vein → SVC
Cardiac TAPVC - pulmonary veins drain to coronary sinus
Mixed TAPVC - different veins drain to different sites
explanation: The 'snowman' or 'figure-of-8' cardiac silhouette on CXR is the classic appearance of SUPRACARDIAC TAPVC. In supracardiac TAPVC (most common type, ~50% of cases), all pulmonary veins join a common pulmonary vein that drains into the LEFT VERTICAL VEIN → innominate vein → SVC → RA. The snowman shape is formed by: the upper portion = dilated left vertical vein + dilated SVC (forming the 'head' of the snowman), and the lower portion = the heart (forming the 'body'). This type is USUALLY UNOBSTRUCTED. Infracardiac TAPVC (20-25%): drains to portal vein/ductus venosus (MOST LIKELY TO BE OBSTRUCTED - presents as emergency). Cardiac TAPVC: drains to coronary sinus or RA directly. ASD/PFO is always present for any systemic output in all types.
highYield: Snowman/figure-of-8 CXR = supracardiac TAPVC (most common type); vertical vein → innominate → SVC; infracardiac type = most often obstructed (emergency)
pyqNote: High-yield CXR association: snowman = supracardiac TAPVC; infracardiac = obstructed = emergency surgery; must know all 4 types

subject: Pediatrics
topic: Congenital Heart Disease - Acyanotic vs Cyanotic
stem: A pediatric cardiology resident is classifying congenital heart defects. Which of the following correctly pairs a CYANOTIC CHD with its defining feature?
options: TOF - most common cyanotic CHD / boot-shaped heart
ASD - fixed wide splitting of S2
explanation: Tetralogy of Fallot (TOF) is the MOST COMMON CYANOTIC congenital heart defect (after the neonatal period), accounting for ~10% of all CHDs. Its defining features include the 4 components (VSD, PS, overriding aorta, RVH), the boot-shaped heart (coeur en sabot) on CXR, and progressive cyanosis. VSD is the most common CHD OVERALL but is ACYANOTIC (left-to-right shunt). ASD with fixed wide splitting of S2 is also ACYANOTIC. PDA with continuous machinery murmur is also ACYANOTIC. Common cyanotic CHDs mnemonic: '5 T's' = TOF (most common after neonatal period), TGA (most common in neonatal period), Truncus arteriosus, Tricuspid atresia, TAPVC.
highYield: TOF = most common cyanotic CHD (after neonatal period); TGA = most common cyanotic CHD in neonates; VSD = most common CHD overall (acyanotic); 5 T's of cyanotic CHD
pyqNote: Extremely high-yield distinction: VSD = most common CHD (acyanotic); TOF = most common CYANOTIC CHD; frequently confused in exams

subject: Pediatrics
topic: Congenital Heart Disease - PS Ejection Click
stem: A 7-year-old child is examined for a murmur. An ejection click is heard at the pulmonary area. The ejection click in pulmonary stenosis has which unique feature that distinguishes it from other ejection clicks?
options: It is louder during expiration and disappears during inspiration
It radiates to the carotid arteries
It is heard only with the bell of the stethoscope
It becomes louder with sitting up position
explanation: The pulmonary ejection click in valvular pulmonary stenosis has a UNIQUE FEATURE: it is LOUDER DURING EXPIRATION and DIMINISHES OR DISAPPEARS DURING INSPIRATION. This is the OPPOSITE of most right-sided cardiac events (which typically increase with inspiration). Reason: During inspiration, the tricuspid valve opens, right ventricular filling increases, and the RV pressure rises, causing the domed pulmonary valve to be partially pre-opened before systole - thus less abrupt opening in systole = softer click. During expiration, the pulmonary valve is not pre-opened, so the abrupt doming in systole produces a loud click. This respiratory variation of the pulmonary ejection click is a classic teaching point. The aortic ejection click does not vary with respiration.
highYield: Pulmonary ejection click = louder in EXPIRATION, softer in INSPIRATION (unique among right-sided events that usually increase with inspiration); due to pre-opening of pulmonic valve in inspiration
pyqNote: Classic auscultation pearl in NEET PG: pulmonary ejection click is the ONLY right-sided auscultatory event that decreases with inspiration

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 35-year-old woman with a large unrepaired ASD (secundum type) presents with palpitations. ECG shows atrial flutter. She has no other symptoms and resting oxygen saturation is 97%. Echocardiography shows right heart dilation. When is surgical/device closure of ASD indicated?
options: All ASDs regardless of size should be closed immediately at diagnosis
ASD closure is indicated for significant shunts (Qp:Qs >1.5:1) with right heart dilation or symptoms, in the absence of severe pulmonary hypertension
ASD closure is contraindicated once the patient is >30 years of age
explanation: ASD closure is indicated when: (1) Hemodynamically significant shunt: Qp:Qs >1.5:1 (pulmonary to systemic flow ratio), (2) Right heart dilation (volume overload of RV), (3) Symptoms (dyspnea, exercise intolerance, atrial arrhythmias like flutter/fibrillation as in this case), (4) Paradoxical embolism. ASD closure is NOT indicated for: trivial ASDs without RV dilation, and is CONTRAINDICATED when there is severe pulmonary hypertension with right-to-left shunting (Eisenmenger). Age is NOT an absolute contraindication - even adults benefit from closure (reduces arrhythmia risk, prevents further RV dilation). Small secundum ASDs (<5mm) may close spontaneously in infancy. Device closure (Amplatzer occluder) is preferred for secundum ASDs with adequate rims. Surgical closure for primum, sinus venosus, and coronary sinus ASDs.
highYield: ASD closure indications: Qp:Qs >1.5:1 + RV dilation or symptoms; device closure (Amplatzer) for secundum; surgery for primum/sinus venosus; contraindicated in Eisenmenger
pyqNote: NEET PG: indications for ASD closure — Qp:Qs ratio, RV dilation, symptoms; important to know what is NOT an indication

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A 6-month-old infant with Down syndrome (Trisomy 21) undergoes echocardiography as part of routine screening. Which congenital heart defect is MOST COMMONLY found in Down syndrome?
explanation: Atrioventricular Septal Defect (AVSD), also called Complete AV Canal (endocardial cushion defect), is the MOST COMMON congenital heart defect in Down syndrome (Trisomy 21), occurring in ~40% of Down syndrome patients with CHD. AVSD involves: a primum ASD + inlet VSD + abnormal AV valves (cleft in mitral and tricuspid leaflets). This defect arises from failure of the endocardial cushions to fuse properly. The second most common in Down syndrome is VSD. ECG in AVSD/complete AV canal shows left axis deviation + RBBB (similar to primum ASD). All neonates and infants with Down syndrome should undergo echocardiography. Trisomy 21 accounts for the majority of endocardial cushion defects.
highYield: Down syndrome + CHD: AVSD (complete AV canal/endocardial cushion defect) is most common; involves primum ASD + inlet VSD + cleft AV valves; ALL Down syndrome infants need echo
pyqNote: Very high-yield NEET PG: most common CHD in Down syndrome = AVSD (not secundum ASD, which is the most common ASD type overall)

subject: Pediatrics
stem: A child born to a mother who had rubella infection in the first trimester is evaluated. On cardiac examination, a continuous murmur is heard. Which congenital heart defect is MOST CHARACTERISTICALLY associated with congenital rubella syndrome?
options: Patent Ductus Arteriosus and peripheral pulmonary artery stenosis
explanation: Congenital Rubella Syndrome (CRS) most characteristically causes Patent Ductus Arteriosus (PDA) and peripheral pulmonary artery stenosis (PPAS). The classic triad of CRS is: (1) Cataracts, (2) Sensorineural deafness, (3) Cardiac defects (PDA and peripheral PA stenosis). The continuous machinery murmur in this child is consistent with PDA. PDA is the most common cardiac manifestation of rubella (along with peripheral pulmonary artery stenosis). Other associations: rubella arthritis, blueberry muffin rash, hepatosplenomegaly, microcephaly, psychomotor delay, 'salt and pepper' retinopathy. Rubella causes CHD through vascular injury (arteritis) affecting the development of the ductus and pulmonary vasculature. This is distinct from TOF (which is NOT characteristically caused by rubella).
highYield: Congenital rubella = PDA + peripheral pulmonary artery stenosis (cardiac); triad = cataracts + deafness + cardiac; PDA most characteristic cardiac defect
pyqNote: NEET PG: rubella → PDA is the most characteristic cardiac defect; distinguish from DiGeorge (truncus/TOF) and Down syndrome (AVSD)
